Facts.text: Paolo Orsini replaces his brother Pope Stephen II as Pope Paul I
King of the Germans Frederick II grants bishops sovereign rights
Italian Renaissance poet Francesco Petrarch famously climbs Mont Ventoux
Korean Confucian scholar and statesman Jeong Mong-ju is assassinated on the Sonjuk Bridge in Gaeseong (now North Korea). A brown spot on the bridge is still said to be his blood.
The miraculous image in Our Lady of Good Counsel appear in Genazzano, Italy
Jamestown expedition makes first landing in America at a place named Cape Henry, in what would become Virginia, but they quickly depart for a better site
Jews are expelled from Brazil
Dutch West Indies Company denies Peter Stuyvesant's desire to exclude Jews from New Amsterdam colony
Emperor Leopold I forms University of Innsbruck
First Russian university opens in Moscow
Sybil Ludington aged 16, rides 40 miles in New York to warn her father's militia of the approach of the British
Meteorites fall in L'Aigle, France
Odd Fellows Lodge forms
Russia declares war on Turkey to support Greece's independence
"Bombay Gazette" begins publishing on silk
Dutch King William III disbands 2nd Chamber
US Congressman Daniel E. Sickles is acquitted in the murder of Philip Barton Key on grounds of "temporary insanity," the first time this defense is used successfully in the US
National Park (later renamed Royal National Park) established south of Sydney, Australia. World's second-oldest national park.
Huntsville Electric Co forms to sell electricity
First Cleveland Board of Park Commissioners forms
Newly renamed American League opener in Cleveland draws 6,500 (still as a minor league, was Western League)
Jimmy Collins, having left the Boston Beaneaters (NL), captains and manages his first game for the Boston Americans (loses 10-6 at Baltimore Orioles)
Bell Telephone Company of Antwerp, Belgium, forms
Cubs Jack McCarthy becomes only major league player to throw out 3 runners at plate in 1 game, all were ends of a double play
Motion pictures begin regular showings at the Orpheum Theater in Honolulu, Hawaii
Jamestown, Virginia Tercentenary Exposition opens
First home run hit at Fenway Park by Hugh Bradley of the Red Sox
Italy secretly signs the "Treaty of London" with Britain, France and Russia, bringing Italy into World War I on the Allied side
Harlow Shapley and Heber D. Curtis hold the "Great Debate" on the nature of nebulae, galaxies, and the size of the universe at the US National Academy of Sciences, Washington, D.C.
Husband and wife team Ludowika & Walter Jakobsson representing Finland, win the pairs skating gold medal at the Antwerp Olympics; Ludowika is the only German-born athlete at the Games
Ice hockey makes its Olympic debut at the Antwerp Games with center Frank Fredrickson scoring 7 goals in Canada's 12-1 drubbing of Sweden in the gold medal match
Pulitzer prize awarded to Edna Ferber for "So big"
Germany and Russia sign a neutrality peace treaty
Karachai Autonomous Region forms in RSFSR (until 1943)
First non-stop England to India flight lands
Jewish students are barred from school in Germany
Frank Boucher is given NHL's Lady Byng Trophy for sportsmanship permanently for winning it 7 of 11 years
German Luftwaffe destroys Basque town of Guernica in Spain
Austrian Jews required to register property above 5,000 Reichsmarks
A tradition begins with the first organ at a baseball stadium (Chicago Cubs)
Potatoes rationed in Holland
Coal mine explosion kills 1,549 at Honkeiko, Manchuria
First B-29 is attacked by Japanese fighters, one fighter shot down
Papandreou government in Greece forms
World War II: Battle of Bautzen - last successful German tank-offensive of the war and last noteworthy victory of the Wehrmacht
Vivian Ellis and A. P. Herbert' musical "Bless the Bride" opena at the Adelphia Theatre in London; runs for 886 performances
Transjordan is officially renamed the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan
Last horse race at Havre de Grace Track in Md, is run
U of Miami ends William & Mary straight tennis match victories at 82
US minesweeper Hobson rams aircraft carrier Wasp, kills 176
Far Eastern Affairs conference opens in Geneva
USSR Supreme Soviet transfer the Crimean oblast from Russian SFSR to Ukrainian SSR
First modern container ship, the Ideal X, leaves Port Newark, New Jersey for Houston, Texas
Jamestown, Virginia 350th Anniversary Festival opens
French paratroopers' revolt suppressed in Algeria
First Lockheed A-12 flight
Ranger 4 crash lands on (backside of) Moon
US and UK launch Ariel, the first UK satellite and the first international payload
Tanganyika and Zanzibar form the United Republic of Tanganyika and Zanzibar
An earthquake of magnitude 7.5 destroys Tashkent, Uzbekistan
International Olympic Committee votes to award German city of Munich the right to host the 1972 Summer Olympic Games
KSPS TV channel 7 in Spokane, WA (PBS) begins broadcasting
San Marco 2 Launch (1st Equatorial Launch)
Students seize administration building at Ohio State
US underground nuclear test, "Boxcar," 1 megaton device
Firestone World Bowling Tournament (Mercury Open) won by Jim Godman
Heaviest rains ever in Bahia district of Brazil, 15" in 24 hrs
San Francisco lightship replaced by automatic buoy
Turkey state of siege proclaimed
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Landslide in Huancavelica Province Peru creates a natural dam
Malta adopts constitution
Yankees trade Peterson, Beene, Kline & Buskey to Indians for Chambliss, Tidrow & Upshaw
Pan Am begins nonstop flights between NYC and Tokyo
Opening of Studio 54 in New York, N.Y.
France sends troops to Chad
NASA launches space vehicle S-201
Gerard Nijboer runs Dutch record marathon (2:09:01)
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Iran begins scattering US hostages from US Embassy
Longest jump by a jet boat is set at 120 feet
Gene Michaelbecomes NY Yankee manager for 2nd time
Dow Jones Industrial Average breaks 1,200 for 1st time
San Antonio spurs beat Denver Nuggets, 152-133 in NBA playoff game
Liverpool's Cavern Club reopens
Experimental aircraft Piasecki PA-97 Helistat - a combination of 4 helicopters and a blimp - crashes during first test flight, killing one pilot, at US Naval Air Station in Lakehurst, New Jersey
Firestone World Bowling Tournament of Champions won by Marshall Holman
France performs nuclear test
Game between Angels & Twins delayed for 9 minutes by strong winds
NBA approves addition of 3rd referee in 1988-89 season
AT&T announces New Jersey's 201 area code will split into 908 and 201
126 die in a (6.9) earthquake in China
Danny Wood of New Kids, steps on a stuffed animal & twists his ankle
NY court of appeals ends 2½ year legal battle over 1988 America's Cup by refusing jurisdiction of case
"Dinosaurs" premieres on ABC-TV
Twenty-three killed in Kansas and Oklahoma by tornadoes
"Growing Pains" final episode on ABC TV
"Who's The Boss" final episode after 8 years on ABC TV
NFL Draft: University of Washington defensive end Steve Emtman from first pick by Indianapolis Colts
Indian Airlines Flight 491 (Boeing 737) crashes at Aurangabad, India, killing 55 and injuring 63
Space Shuttle Columbia, mission STS-55, launches into orbit carrying Spacelab D2 laboratory
26.9°C in Prestebakke Norway (Norwegian April high temperature record)
China Airlines Flight 140 crashes in Nagoya, Japan, killing 264 of the 271 passengers
First day of voting in the first-ever multiracial elections in South Africa, Dr. Nomaza Paintin in New Zealand is the first Black South African to vote
Physicists announce the first evidence of the top quark subatomic particle
Baseball season begins after lengthy strike
Coors Field, opens in Denver, Rockies beat Mets 11-9 in 14 innings
Shaun Pollock takes 4 wkts in 4 balls for Warwickshire in B&H
BBC presenter Jill Dando shot and killed outside her London home - launches Metropolitan Police's largest murder enquiry,  crime remains unsolved
Robert Steinhäuser infiltrates and kills 17 at Gutenberg-Gymnasium in Erfurt, Germany before dying of a self-inflicted gunshot.
NFL Draft: USC quarterback Carson Palmer first pick by Cincinnati Bengals
Under international pressure, Syria withdraws the last of its 14,000 troops from Lebanon, ending its 29-year military domination of the country
Queen's Pier is officially closed by the Hong Kong government to facilitate land reclamation in Hong Kong's Central district after a bitter struggle by conservationists
NFL Draft: University of Michigan offensive tackle Jake Long first pick by Miami Dolphins
70 people are killed by rocket attacks by the Syrian Army on the city of Hama
Indonesia suspends imports of American beef after a confirmed case of mad cow disease in California
NFL Draft: Stanford quarterback Andrew Luck first pick by Indianapolis Colts
30 people are killed after a bus crashes following a Taliban attack in southern Afghanistan
Oratorio for chorus evoking Pennsylvania coal-mining life  "Anthracite Fields" by composer Julia Wolfe premieres in Philadelphia (Pulitzer Prize for Music 2015)
CEO of Chobani, Hamdi Ulukaya, announces that he will be giving his employees 10% of the shares in Chobani
Gift Ngoepe becomes 1st black African to play in Major League Baseball, for the Pittsburgh Pirates
NFL Draft: Oklahoma quarterback Baker Mayfield first pick by Cleveland Browns; 4 QBs taken in top 10 selections for first time in history
Serial killer "Golden State Killer" identified after 40 years as a former police officer, responsible for 12 killings, 50 rapes in California
"No religion" tops survey of American religious identity for the first time at 23.1% edging out Catholics 23.0% and evangelicals 22.5%, in long-running General Social Survey
Six suspected militants connected to Sri Lankan terror attacks killed along with ten others in a shootout with police in Sainthamaruthu
Waorani people of Pastaza win landmark environmental case against the Ecuadorian government to protect half a million acres of their territory in the Amazon rainforest
The US, the EU and other countries announce they are sending pandemic aid to India as it's COVID-19 crisis continues to worsen
US Census results shows its population growth second slowest in recorded history, population at 331,449,281 with only 7.4% increase on 2010
Russia says it will stop supplying gas to Poland and Bulgaria, after the countries refused to pay in rubles, escalating the energy supply standoff between Russia and Europe
World Bank warns the war in Ukraine will cause the "largest commodity shock" since the 1970s, with large economic and humanitarian effects
106 tornadoes tear through  Nebraska, Iowa, Texas, Kansas and Missouri destroying hundreds of homes
Pazzi conspirators attackLorenzo de' Mediciand kill Giuliano de' Medici in Florence
Copernicusmakes his 1st observations of Saturn
William Shakespeareis baptized in Stratford-upon-Avon, Warwickshire, England
KingLouis XVIIIlands at Calais from England
Frédéric Chopin's "Grand Polonaise Brillante" premieres in Paris
Alfred Russel Wallacedeparts the UK for South America, beginning four years of travel, collecting, and research in the region
ComposerGioachino Rossinileaves Italy for the final time, returning to Paris, France
Confederate GeneralJoseph E. Johnstonsurrenders remaining forces to Union GeneralWilliam T. Shermanat Bennett Place in Durham, North Carolina, ending the American Civil War
Sun Yet San calls for revolt against PresidentYuan Shikaiin China
ArtistDiego Riveraresigns from the Mexican Communist Party
Madame Tussaud's waxwork exhibition reopens in London after a fire
Lou Gehrighits a HR but is called out for passing a runner, mistake costs him AL home run crown; he &Babe Ruthtie for season
Jean Anouilh's play "L'Hermine (The Ermine)" premieres in Paris
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 4th Symphony
MarshalPhilippe Pétain, leader of France's Vichy collaborationist regime during World War II, is arrested for treason
Queen Julianaof the Netherlands opens Brielsche Mausoleum
Patty Bergscores 64, the best competitive round of golf by a woman
"Seven Samurai", Japanese film directed byAkira Kurosawa, starringToshiro Mifune, is released
New York Yankees outfielderRoger Marishits 1st of 61 homers in 1961
LPGA Titleholders Championship Women's Golf, Augusta CC: Marilynn Smith retains title; beatsMickey Wrightby 1 stroke
Charles Ives' 4th Symphony premieres at Carnegie Hall, New York, 11 years after the composer's death
Red Auerbachretires as Boston Celtic's coach
MLB Philadelphia Phillies sluggerMike Schmidt's 2 home runs in 7-3 win over Pirates at Three Rivers Stadium in Pittsburgh ties NL record of 11 HRs in April
MLB Philadelphia PhilliesSteve Carltonpitches his 6th 1-hitter, in 7-0 win over St. Louis Cardinals, at Veterans Stadium; 1st National League to record 6 1-hitters
Rod Stewartis mugged, gunman steals his $50,000 Porsche on Hollywood Boulevard, Los Angeles, California
NFL Draft: Stanford quarterbackJohn Elwayfirst pick by Baltimore Colts
US PresidentRonald Reaganvisits China
1st TNN Viewers choice awards:Randy Traviswins in 5 categories
NY Mets Davey Johnson becomes 2nd manager in MLB history to record 400 victory in 1st 4 years (Al Lópezwas 1st)
Mike Tysongets a speeding ticket for drag racing his Lamborghini in Albany, New York
Texas RangersNolan RyantiesBob Feller's record of pitching 12 one-hitters in 1-0 win over Chicago White Sox at Arlington Stadium
Soccer starDiego Maradona, suspended for using cocaine, arrested in Argentina for possession & distribution of illegal narcotics
Alex Haley(Roots) wins 1992 Ellis Island Award, posthumously
MLB St. Louis CardinalsOzzie Smithsteals his 500th base
NBC announcesConan O'Briento replaceDavid Lettermanon "Late Night"
TV series "Due South" about Canadian Mounties starringPaul Grosspremieres in Canada
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's 13th studio album "Devils and Dust"
"Iron Man 2", directed byJon Favreau, starringRobert Downey Jr. andGwyneth Paltrow, premieres in Los Angeles
US TV singing competition "The Voice" premieres with judgesBlake Shelton, Adam Levine,Christina Aguileraand CeeLo Green
Cory Monteithis reported to have completed treatment at a drug rehabilitation facility
35th London Marathon: KenyanEliud Kipchoge& Ethiopian Tigist Tufa win
FC Bayern Munich wins the 2014–15 Bundesliga for a 25th time
Nursultan Nazarbayevis re-elected President of Kazakhstan with 97.7% of the vote
"The Handmaid's Tale" debuts on Hulu starringElisabeth Moss,Joseph Fiennesand Yvonne Strahovski, based on the book byMargaret Atwood
ComedianBill Cosbyis found guilty of sexual assault in Pennsylvania
Kanye West's Nike Air Yeezy 1 Prototype trainers sell for a record $1.8 million in a private sale at Sotheby's
President Recep TayyipErdoganannounces Turkey will go into full lockdown till May 17 to a curb COVID-19 surge and world's 4th highest caseload
US infectious diseases expert Dr.Anthony Faucisays that the country is out of the "Pandemic Phase" - for the moment
Brazilian Portuguese-language Michaelis dictionary adds "pelé" as a new adjective to its online edition, meaning "exceptional, incomparable, unique"
US PresidentJoe Bidenannounces his bid for a second term saying he has a “job to finish”
Writer E. Jean Carroll testifies in a NY court thatDonald Trumpraped her in a department store in the 1990s
Pope Francisis buried at Rome's Papal Basilica of Saint Mary Major after a Vatican ceremony; hundreds of thousands line the streets and fill Saint Peter's Square to pay tribute during the funeral
General Kuroko leads the Japanese Army against the large Russian force at the Yalu river during the Russo-Japanese War
Mass trials ofJonas Salk's anti-polio vaccine begin; the first shot is delivered in Fairfax County, Virginia; more than 443,000 children receive shots over three months
Argentina surrenders to British forces on South Georgia Island, near the Falkland Islands

The world's worst nuclear disaster occurs when the fourth reactor at the Chernobyl nuclear power station in the Soviet Union explodes, resulting in 31 deaths and radioactive contamination spreading to much of Western Europe
Gian Paolo Lomazzo, Italian painter and poet (Trattato), born in Milan (d. 1600)
Nicolas Formé, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1638)
Marie de' Medici, Queen of France, born in Palazzo Pitti, Florence (d. 1642)

Philip Julius van Lichtenbergh, Governor of Suriname, born in Heusden, Noord-Brabant, Netherlands (d. 1674)

Marie Louise D'Orleans, Queen consort of Spain as the first wife of King Charles II of Spain, born in Palais-Royal, Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1689)
Johannes Burman, Dutch botanist, plant author and physician, born in Amsterdam (d. 1779)
Thomas Reid, Scottish philosopher and founder of Scottish School of Common Sense, born in Strachan, Scotland (d. 1796)
Esek Hopkins, American Revolutionary War admiral and Commander in Chief of the Continental Navy, born in Scituate, Rhode Island (d. 1802)
Pasquale Paoli, Corsican freedom fighter, born in Stretta di Morosaglia, France (d. 1807)
Christian Leopold von Buch, German geologist and paleontologist, born in Stolpe an der Oder, Margraviate of Brandenburg, Prussia (d. 1853)
Joan Melchior Kemper, Dutch lawyer (designed civil code law book), born in Amsterdam (d. 1824)
Maria Amalia of Bourbon-Sicily, French queen as wife of Louis Phillip of Austria, born in Caserta Palace, Naples (d. 1866)
Ludwig Uhland, German writer, born in Tübingen, Württemberg (d. 1862)
Auguste Mathieu Panseron, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1859)
Eugène Delacroix, French painter and etcher (Journal), born in Charenton-Saint-Maurice, Île-de-France, France (d. 1863)

Ambrose Dudley Mann, American diplomat, born in Hanover Courthouse, Virginia (d. 1889)

Karl Ludwig Friedrich Hetsch, German composer, born in Stuttgart, Kingdom of Württemberg (d. 1872)
Martha Finley, American children's book author (Elsie Dinsmore series), born in Chillicothe, Ohio (d. 1909)
Alfred Krupp, German steel merchant and largest arms manufacturer of his day, born in Essen, Germany (d. 1887)
Alice Cary, American poet (Cincinnati Sentinel), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1871)
Jan Albert van Eijken, Dutch composer and organist, born in Amersfoort, Netherlands (d. 1868)

George Hull Ward, American general and Union officer in the American Civil War, born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1863)
Charles Edward Hovey, American lobbyist and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Thetford, Vermont (d. 1897)
Artemus Ward [Charles Farrar Browne], American humorist (considered to be America's first stand-up comedian), born in Waterford, Maine (d. 1867)
Horatio Richmond Palmer, American composer and choir director, born in Sherburne, New York (d. 1907)
Erminnie Adelle Platt, American anthropologist (first woman to specialize in ethnographic field work), born in Marcellus shale, New York (d. 1886)
Cyrus Hamblin, American attorney, politician, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Hampden, Maine (d. 1867)
Wilhelm Scherer, German literature historian, born in Schönborn, Austrian Empire (d. 1886)
Giuseppe Arimondi, Italian general who fought in the Italo-Ethiopian War, born in Savigliano (d. 1896)
Joseph Ward, 17th Prime Minister of New Zealand (1906-12), born in Melbourne, Victoria Colony (d. 1930)
Arno Holz, German writer (Phantasus), born in Rastenburg, East Prussia (d. 1929)
Charles Haslewood Shannon, English lithographer and painter, born in Sleaford, Lincolnshire (d. 1937)
Akseli Gallen-Kallela, Finnish painter known for his illustrations of the Kalevala, the Finnish national epic, born in Pori, Finland (d. 1931)[1]
Harold Sidney Harmsworth, 1st Viscount Rothermere, English newspaper (Daily Mail and Daily Mirror), born in London, England (d. 1940)
William Desmond Taylor [Deane-Tanner], Irish director and actor (The Awakening, Broken Coin), born in County Carlow, Ireland (d. 1922)
Otto zur Linde, German author (Charon), born in Essen, Germany (d. 1938)
Biddy Anderson, South African cricket batsman (1 Test as captain; Western Province) and rugby union centre (3 Tests), born in Kimberley, South Africa (d. 1926)
Ethel Griffies, British actress (Billy Liar, The Birds, We Live Again), born in Sheffield, Yorkshire, England (d. 1975)
Rafael Guízar y Valencia, Mexican Catholic bishop (cared for the wounded, sick, and dying during the Mexican Revolution), born in Cotija, Michoacán, Mexico (d. 1938)
Owen Williams Richardson, British physicist (Nobel 1928), born in Dewsbury, Yorkshire, England (d. 1959)
Mikhail Fokine, Russian choreographer and founder of modern dance, born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1942)
Harry Sothern, British actor (Blind Wives, Buck Rogers), born in London, England (d. 1957)
Ğabdulla Tuqay, Tatar poet (Oh My Mother Tongue!), born in Quşlawıç, Kazan Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1913)
Ma Rainey [Gertrude Pridgett], American singer known as the "Mother of the Blues", born in Columbus, Georgia (d. 1939)
Anita Loos, American screenwriter, novelist (Gentlemen Prefer Blondes) and playwright, born in Sisson, California (d. 1981)
Mahmoud El Nokrashy Pasha, Egyptian politician and the 27th Prime Minister of Egypt (1946-48) who was later assassinated, born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 1948)

Edgar Kennedy, American silent and sound screen character actor famous for his 'slow burn' (Laurel & Hardy films; Duck Soup; "Average Man" shorts) and director (From Soup to Nuts), born in Lake San Antonio, California (d. 1948)
Ernst Udet, German WWI pilot and notable flying ace who helped develop the Luftwaffe under the Nazi Party, born in Frankfurt am Main, German Empire (d. 1941)

John Grierson, Scottish documentary filmmaker (Drifters), born in Deanston, Stirling, Scotland (d. 1972)
Vicente Aleixandre, Spanish writer (Ambito, Bird of Paper, Nobel 1977), born in Seville, Spain (d. 1984)

Charles F. Richter, American physicist and seismologist (developed the Richter scale for measuring earthquake magnitude), born in Overpeck, Ohio (d. 1985)
Niven Busch, American screenwriter (The Postman Always Rings Twice), born in New York City (d. 1991)
Paul-Émile Léger, Canadian Cardinal of the Roman Catholic Church and Archbishop (Montreal), born in Salaberry-de-Valleyfield, Quebec, Canada (d. 1991)
Charles K. Feldman, American producer (Casino Royale), born in New York City (d. 1968)
Denis O'Dea, Irish actor (Niagara, Esther & the King, Treasure Island), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1978)
Jean Vigo, French actor and director (Zero For Conduct, L'Atlante), born in Paris, France (d. 1934)
Theun de Vries, Dutch writer (The Girl with the Red Hair), born in Veenwouden, Friesland, Netherlands (d. 2005)
Dick Howorth, English cricket all-rounder (5 Tests, 19 wickets; Worcestershire CCC), born in Bacup, England (d. 1980)
Erland von Koch, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 2009)
Ernst Tittel, Austrian organist and composer, born in Sternberg, Moravia (d. 1969)
Johan Hermanus Doorn, Dutch journalist and resistance fighter (Orange Newspaper), born in Amsterdam (d. 1944)
Ruan Lingyu [Fenggen], Chinese silent film actress (The Goddess), born in Shanghai (d. 1935)
Tomoyuki Tanaka, Japanese film producer (Godzilla), born in Ōsaka, Japan (d. 1997)
Marianne Hoppe, German theater and film actress (Der Schimmelreiter), born in Rostock, Germany (d. 2002)
A. E. van Vogt, Canadian sci-fi author (Wizard of Linn), born in Gretna, Manitoba (d. 2000)
Bernard Malamud, American novelist (The Fixer, The Natural, Pulitzer 1967), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1986)
Charlie Chester, English comedian (Never Say Die), born in Eastbourne, Sussex (d. 1997)
H. L. Gold, American sci-fi writer and editor, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1996)
James Rouse, American builder (founder of The Rouse Company), born in Easton, Maryland (d. 1996)
Wilfrid Howard Mellers, English musicologist (François Couperin and the French Classical Tradition), and composer, born in Leamington Spa, Warwickshire, England (d. 2008)
Johnny Shines, American delta blues singer and guitarist, born in Memphis, Tenneessee (d. 1992)
Arnold van Wyk, South African concert pianist and composer (Nagmusiek - Night Music), born in Calvinia, Cape Province, Union of South Africa (d. 1983)
Morris West, Australian novelist (Shoes of the Fisherman), born in St Kilda, Victoria (d. 1999)
Vic Perrin, American radio and television actor (Dragnet), and screenwriter (Gunsmoke), born in Menomonee Falls, Wisconsin (d. 1989)
Virgil Trucks, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1945 Detroit Tigers; MLB All Star 1949, 54), born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 2013)
Werner Bischof, Swiss photographer (Magnum), born in Zurich, Switzerland (d. 1954)
Sal Maglie, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1954; MLB All Star 1951, 52; NY Giants; no-hitter 1956 Cleveland Indians), born in Niagara Falls, New York (d. 1992)
Francina "Fanny" Blankers-Koen, Dutch athlete (Olympic gold 100m, 200m, 80m hurdles, 4x100m relay 1948), born in Lage Vuursche, Netherlands (d. 2004)
Stafford Repp, American actor (Playhouse 90, Batman, Plunder Road), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1974)

Padú del Caribe [Juan Chabaya Lampe], Aruban pianist and composer, known as the "Father of the Caribbean" ("Aruba Dushi Tera" - Aruba's national anthem), born in Oranjestad, Aruba, Kingdom of the Netherlands (d. 2019)

Mike Kellin [Myron Kellin], American actor (Pipe Dream, American Buffalo), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1983)

Jørgen Ingmann, Danish jazz and pop guitarist ("Apache"; "Dansevise"), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2015)
Michele Ferrero, Italian entrepreneur (Ferrero SpA - Nutella), born in Dogliani, Piedmont (d. 2015)
Wilma Lipp, Austrian soprano (Vienna Opera, 1945-81; Mozart's Die Zauberflöte - "Queen of the Night"), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2019)

Michael Mathias Prechtl, German illustrator, born in Amberg, Bavaria (d. 2003)
Oldřich František Korte, Czech pianist, composer, and writer, born in Šaľa, Slovakia (d. 2014)
Preston Robert Tisch, American businessman (co-founder of Loews Corp), and NFL co-owner (NY Giants, 1991-2005), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Dame Anne McLaren, British biologist and leading researcher on human in vitro fertilisation (IVF), born in London, England (d. 2007)
Jack Douglas [John Douglas Robertson], English actor (Carry On films), born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1989)
John Ralston, American College Football Hall of Fame linebacker, coach and executive (Cal, Stanford, Denver Broncos), born in Oakland, California (d. 2019)
Richard Mitchell, American professor and author (The Underground Grammarian), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2002)
Derek Waring [Barton-Chapple], British actor (Z-Cars, Barnacle Bill, Arthur-Heart of the Country), born in Mill Hill, London (d. 2007)
Roger Moens, Belgian middle distance runner (800m world record 1955), born in Aalst, Belgium
Bernie Brillstein, American talent agent, manager and producer (The Days and Nights of Molly Dodd), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2008)
Francis Lai, French film music composer (Love Story), born in Nice, Alpes-Maritimes, France (d. 2018)
Michael Smith, English-Canadian biochemist (Nobel 1993- Site-directed mutagenesis), born in Blackpool, England (d. 2000)
Arno Penzias, German-born American physicist (1978 Nobel Prize for detecting cosmic microwave background), born in Munich, Germany (d. 2024)[1]
Filiberto Ojeda Ríos, Puerto Rican activist (Founder of FALN), born in Río Blanco, Naguabo, Puerto Rico (d. 2005)
Ilkka Kuusisto, Finnish opera composer, organist, arts administrator (Finnish National Opera, 1984-92), broadcaster, and educator, born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 2025)
Donald Sterling, American attorney and businessman, owner of the San Diego/Los Angeles Clippers (1981-2014), born in Chicago, Illinois
Esteban Sánchez, Spanish classical pianist and pedagogue (Badajoz Conservatoire, 1978-97), born in Orellana la Vieja, Spain (d. 1997)

James Cecil "Humpy" Parker, American disgraced ex-Sheriff convicted of violating inmates civil rights and of torture, born in San Jacinto County, Texas (d. 1994)
Doug Sax, American mastering engineer (Bob Dylan, The Doors, Pink Floyd, Ray Charles), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2015)
